item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Honestly, I like it, but it’s kind of confusing in some parts.
Neutral vote

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sorry for the necropost, but you'd expressed plans to revisit this point. Still interested?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ayup. I feex.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Shouldn't this be tagged as "ranine"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think that, by this point, they'd be referring to them as SCP-2655-1, SCP-2655-2, etc.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm down voting for the same reasons others have stated: it's confusingly designated, a little off in tone from time to time, and ultimately just a little bit too boring. That said, it's very high standard for a first SCP, and I urge you to keep reading and keep writing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No vote. The article as a whole is pretty good, and the objects are interesting. Reason for no vote, backing up everything Aelanna said. Also it's almost a down vote, because of the first note. I really do not like the way that reads.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Aside from some quirks, it's a pretty decent SCP for a first try.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I didn't really care for the notes, but the rest of the article appealed to me enough for an upvote.

Also I find the name "spring peeper" really amusing, for some reason. Heh, heh. Peeper.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's…actually a really good point.

I'm really tired at the moment so I won't screw with that now, but I'll definitely edit for that.

Thanks for bringing that up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is exactly what I was going to comment on. What was the kid doing in-site?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like how, in the Description, you made it seem like SCP-2655 was still what it originally was before "death." I also like the vagueness and ambiguity about what 2655 really is. At first, it was a frog. At the end, it was something much more mysterious. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Looking back on it that is a little awkwardly phrased.

I'll see what I can do.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-2655 is found inside Jack ███████'s pants pocket by Site Security B2. Jack has handled the frog for approximately ten seconds prior to B2's arrival.

I read it as he was right there, on-site at least, when the frog died. Either that, or they had a really compelling reason to go straight back to him to find it. It just sounds like they went, "Oops, frog's dead, better search the kid!" and did that almost immediately.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Got damn… I really like this one for some reason. And the fact that it's your first skip just adds to that. Good job! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
He wasn't supposed to be in containment.

Where does it seem like he is? I'll patch that up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like what you've got going on here, with the numbers and the increase in radiation as well as its growth, but this needs some work. I'd drop the budgetary issues, personally, I thought that was a little odd (not to mention nothing that's got any kind of previous basis). "Crucifer" should not be capitalized and some of the language could use cleaning up. I second removing Jack's first name. I also question why he was in containment when the original 2655 died.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It is biologically identical to any other member of its species, save for a numeral 3 written on its back in an unknown ink.

It is hypothesized that this effect has been present since birth, as no markings or defects to indicate a surgical procedure or experimentation are present.

I found this inconsistent and it threw me off immediately. A big #3 on an anomalous radioactive organism absolutely would be a marking that implied experimentation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's a lot I don't like about this.

The concept isn't really interesting to me. Successive things what get super-radioactive. The fact that it's apparently someone's experiment or something doesn't really improve it for me. The implications are pretty much that the Foundation will have to just maintain a somewhat difficult radioactive object forever. I am not grabbed.

The tone is sloppy. The fact that you refer to the skip by name, terming each new instance an "incarnation", this feels informal and kind of slapdash.

I also don't like the notes. As someone who works in a bureacracy, I appreciate attempts to make the Foundation feel like a real organization when people write. The caveat to that, though, is that you need to be realistic when you do this, otherwise it just takes the piece into "HURR DURR Incompetent Foundation" territory. In this case, the guy being all like "I'm gonna reclassify this for more money" is completely ridiculous. Any organization as complex as the Foundation is going to have a far better budgetary process than "lamely game the system for your projects".

This just doesn't work on any level for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm novoting for these reasons. Also, a little bit of awkward wording.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Narrow downvote. A lot of the problems have been ironed out, but the notes add an air of obviousness that thoroughly explains the anomaly a little too much. Also, I'm still not quite seeing the connection between the various iterations, so it's a little convenient that successive numbers were a frog and the owner.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A lot of the wording is bothering me:

  • What's with the cell measurements?
  • "termination" refers to an intentional severance of employment (or life). If something dies of natural causes, it simply dies. It isn't terminated.
  • Similarly, the use of "has been neutralized" is awkward. We would usually say that "SCP-2655 has died, and been declared Neutralized" (note capitalization of object subclass).
  • This is a case where you really should have been using subobject notation: the frog should be SCP-2655-1, Jack should be SCP-2655-2, and the infected researcher should be SCP-2655-3. Or whatever.
  • "SCP-2655's Neutralized status is revoked, and containment is reaffirmed" should probably be "SCP-2655's Neutralized status has been rescinded and its containment procedures have been reinstated".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is really your first? I'm impressed! You have my vote. And my sword. And my axe.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very interesting article, and quite well executed. Good job. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nothing much to say about this one, written well, but kind of boring. Neutral vote

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
By accident, that's why :P

As per request, note B is completely gone now.

Well when I get home, it will be. Mobile and wiki editing don't mix.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it. Short, sweet, and pretty interesting. Note B was a little strange though. Why did you have Dr. Kolibri sign his name twice in one note?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have to concur with Anaxagoras on this one. Note B doesn't really do that much for the article.

Also, I would advise fully blanking out the name of Jack Blackbox. It doesn't seem like the Foundation would record his name and use it, especially after he becomes the new SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Note B seems a little silly. It's pretty obvious to the reader where this is going, so note B seems a bit excessively blind.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First thing I noticed is that I was somewhat confused by the dates. At times I thought it started discussing something occurring in the past before transitioning back to the present tense. By the end of the article I had figured it out, but during the reading I was confused. Just something to note.

Also, in the second you have it starting with Dr. Kolibri and then ending with Dr. Kolibri. I recommend removing the first iteration.

Final note: is the SCP itself subject to the debilitating effects of the radiation, and that is what kills it? Or does it just keep producing radiation that has no effect on itself until it dies of old age?

In all, I like the article, but I think it could use some touch ups.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I kinda like it. Raises some good questions, like who is making those numbers. This is quite nice for your first.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is my first SCP!

Special thanks to weizhong, Leahfy, Bryx, Riemann, and…um…everyone else in the chat/on the forums/on the subreddit who gave feedback.

Changelog:
-v2-

-removed note B
